content.full_text:
Farn PoLrrrcAL PnecrrcEs CorraMrssroN
428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramenro, CA 95914-2329
(916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886
August 22,2003
Bruce C. Cline
Office of the CityAttomey
City of Folsom
50 Natoma Street
Folsom, CA 95630
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. A-03-110
Dear Mr. Cline:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Historic District
Commissioner Jeff Ferreira-Pro regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1
QUESTTON
May Commissioner Ferreira-Pro participate in a decision regarding an advisory
recommendation on how the railroad block in the historic district should be developed if
his residence is located within 500 feet of the railroad block?
CONCLUSION
It is presumed that Commissioner Ferreira-Pro has a conflict of interest in this
decision.
FACTS
The Historic District Commission ("HDC") is an appointed commission that
serves as the planning commission for the historic district in Folsom. It is governed by
Folsorn Municipal Code Chapter 11.52. The HDC is responsible for land use decisions in
a specifically defined areathat encompasses approximately 1,568 lots covering
I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations.
if4rg9


File No. 4-03-110
Page No. 2
approximately 98 blocks. The membership of the HDC is defined by ordinance. The
ordinance requires the commission to be comprised of the following:
"A. One representative actively involved with historic preservation;
B. One representative who is a resident of the Historic District;
C. One representative who owns a business within the Sutter Street Subarea;
D. Two Planning Commissioners;
E. One representative from a Historic District business outside the Sutter Street
Subarea; and
F. One architect, landscape architect, or design professional with expertise in
historic preservation. "
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro is presently the chair of the HDC, nominated as the
chair by his fellow commissioners. He sits on the HDC by virtue of his residence, in
which he owns a single family home in the historic district; as such, he is the appointed
"representative who is a resident of the historic district." The commissioner's residence
is within 500 feet of a portion of alarge parcel of city property known as the railroad
block. The railroad block is largely undeveloped and presently used for parking. There
are a number of proposals regarding how the railroad block will be developed and these
proposals will be presented to the HDC for advisory recofilmendations before a final
decision by the city council. In some cases,land use actions necessary for development
of the railroad block may be presented to the HDC for final action.
The railroad block comprises an area of approximately 6.7 acres. The cost of
development in this area will be millions of dollars. The city, the HDC and the people
who live and work in the historic diskict place significant importance on this project. It
is a very important economic development project for the city and important.to the
historic character of the city. The railroad block project and proposed uses are in the
early planning stages, and the prospects of developing this area, and the many ideas for
development, elicit many reactions. The commissioner advises that based on his
perception and some comments received regarding the project, the potential effects of the
project will have a dekimental effect on property values. Based on your review, there are
at least 135 other property owners also within 500 feet of the railroad block.
Additionally, the commissioner advises that other potential effects of the project
include, ambiance of the neighborhoods in the historic district; continuation of the
historic continuity and character of the area, including the aesthetic appearance; traffic
impacts; and noise impacts.
Pursuant to Ordinance No. 890 reenacting Chapter 17.52, among the identified
purposes relating to the historic district are:
"1. To preserve and enhance the historic, small-town atmosphere of the
Historic District as it developed between the years 1850 and 1950;


FileNo. A-03-110
Page No. 3
2. To maintain, restore, and reconstruct historic structures and sites within
the Historic District'
3. To encourage an active business climate which promotes the
development of a diverse range of businesses compatible with the Historic
District as it developed between the years 1850 and 1950;
4. To retain the residential areas within the Historic District;
5. To ensure that new residential and commercial development is
consistent with the historical character of the Historic District as it
developed between the years 1850 and 1950;
6. To increase the awareness, understanding, and appreciation of the
history of the City; and
7. To preserve and enhance open space areas."
The general rule for all members is that eligibility for membership on the
commission includes being a resident and registered voter of the City of Folsom, among
other criteria. If amember of the commission moves his or her residence outside of the
city, the member will be removed from office. Members of the historic district are
required to file a statement of economic interests under the city's conflict of interest code.
ANALYSIS
The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perforrn their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section 81001O).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2
Steps One and Two: Is Jeff Ferreira-Pro considered a 6spublic official,, and is he
making, partieipating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the historic district in the City of Folsom, Jeff Ferreira-Pro is a
"member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agenct'' and,
therefore, is a public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048; regulation 1 870 1 (a).)
' These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regulation 18700(b).


File No. 4-03-110
Page No. 4
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1.)
commissioner Ferreira-Pro will "make a goveflrmental decision" if he
participates in a decision regarding an advisory recoilrmendation on how the railroad
block in the historic district of Folsom should be developed. Additionally, ifhe engages
in any of the actions described in enclosed regulations 18702.2 and 18702.3 with regard
to this decision, he will "participate in making" or'tnfluence', that decision.
Step Three: What is Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest - 
the possible
source ofa conflict ofinterest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));
A public,official has an economic interest in real property in which he or.she.has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);
a
o
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 1 3703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87I03; regulation 13703.5).
An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the
spouse of an official orby a mernber of the official's immediate family, by an agent on
behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the
official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a


File No. 4-03-110
Page No. 5
1O-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family''is defined at section
82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
Provided he has an interest of $2,000 or more in his residence, the commissioner
has an economic interest in this property.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests of
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's. For purposes of this letter, we assume that he has no other
economic interests relevant to the decisions you have identified.
Step Four: Is Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest directly or indirectly
involved in the goyernmental decision?
Real property is directly involved in a govemmental decision if that real property
is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject ofthe governmental decision. (Regulationlg704.2talti).)
You have stated that the decision is regarding the future development of the
railroad block. As such, the railroad block area is the subject of the decision, and the
commissioner's property will be directly involved in the decision since it is located
within 500 feet of this area.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standards of regulation rclfiS.Z(a)
apply. (Regulation 187 04.2(cX 1 ).)
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property which is directly involved in the govemmental decision is
presumed to be material. "This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on
the real property." (Ibid.) Please note that "any financial effect" includes as little as a
penny's worth.
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest will meet the
applicable materiality standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibllity. Qn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
It is presumed that the financial effect of this decision on the property owned by
the commissioner is material and that he has a conflict of interest in this decision. As a


File No. 4-03-110
Page No. 6
result, he is prohibited from participating in this decision unless he can rebut this
presumption or if an exception applies.
In order to rebut the presumption of regulation 18705.2(a)(1), Commissioner
Ferreira-Pro must show thatitis not reasonably foreseeable that the decision (i.e., the
decision to develop the railroad block) will have any financial effect on his property.
However, glven the purposes specified in Ordinance No. 890 pertainjng to the historic
district, it appears that it is unlikely that this presumption will be rebutted.
However, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providin g advice. (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination of whether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest is necessarily afact:ual question that is
ultimately for the commissioner to decide.
Step Seven: The "Public Generally" Exception
Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interest will
experience amateial financial effect as a result of the decision before the official, he or
she may still participate if the "public generally'' exception applies to each person or real
property triggering a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18707@)(4).) Regulation 18707.1
provides the requirements for the general exception:
"(a) Except as provided in Government Code sections
87102.6 and 87103.5, the material financial effect of a
governmental decision on apublic official's economic
interests is indistinguis,lpple from its effect on the public
generally if both subdivisions (b)(1) and (b)(2) of this
regulation apply.
(b) Significant Segments and Indistinguishable Effects.
(1) Significant Segment. The governmental decision will
afFect a 'significant segment' of the public generally if any
of the following are affected as set forth below:
(A) Individuals. For decisions that affect the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of a public official or
a member of his or her immediate family, or that affect an
individual who is a source of income or a source of gifts to
a public official, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the
official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction.
(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect a public
official's real property interest, the decision also affects:


File No. 4-03-110
Page No. 7
(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency.
(C) Business Entities. For decisions that affect a business
entity in which a public official has an economic interest
the decision also affects 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all
business entities in the jurisdiction or the district the
official represents, so long as the effect is on persons
composed of more than a single industry, trade, or
profession. For pu{poses of this subdivision, a not for
profit entity other than a govemmental entity is treated as a
business entity.
fl...11
(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The govemmental
decision will affect a public official's economic interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect the
significant segment identified in subdivision (b)(l) of this
regulation."
Sienificant Seqrnent
Regulation 18707.1(b)(1XB) is the "significant segment" standard used when the
decision affects an official's real property. As stated above, to qualiff as a significant segment
under regulation 18707.1O)(1XB), the decision has to also affect "[t]en percent or more of all
property owners or all homeowners in thejurisdiction of the official's agency or the diskict the
official represents; or (ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
We have previously advised that aplanning commissioner's jurisdiction is the
city. (In re owen (1976) 2 FPPC aps.77; Furth Advice Letter, No. A-99-035; Mertng
Advice Letter, No. I-91-091.) In the Owen opinion, supra,the Commission assessed
whether the "public generally''exception applied to several city council members and
planning commissioners, statingthat the phrase "public generally'' was comprised of the
residents and persons doing business in the City of Davis.
Because all residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of the official, these
residents in the agency's jurisdiction comprise the "public ." (In re Legan (1985) 9 FppC
Ops. 1.) In addition, the Commission concluded that, with regard to county supervisors,
the "public" would consist of the entire county, even though its primary land use
jurisdiction is confined to the unincorporated areas of the county. (In re Legan (1985) 9
FPPC Ops. l.)
You have provided information that the City of Folsom created the HDC and that
the general rule for membership eligibility on the commission includes heing a resident


FileNo. 4-03-110
Page No. 8
and registered voter in the City of Folsom. In addition, if a member of the commission
moves his or her residence outside of the city, the member will be removed from office.
Furthermore, members of the historic district are required to file a statement of economic
interests under the city's conflict of interest code. Based on these facts, we conclude that
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's jurisdiction is the City of Folsom.
As a result, the commissioner must analyze whether the "prrblic generally''
exception applies based on the jurisdiction ofthe City of Folsom.3 You have stated that
the historic district encompasses approximately 1,568 lots. Assuming that each of these
lots is a residential lot owned by a different property owner and that each lot will be
affected by the decision reTatingto the historic district, the first prong (i.e., the
"significant segment" prong) will be met if this number amounts to 10Yo of all property
owners or homeowners in Folsom. You have not provided facts showing that this is the
case. Therefore, we cannot conclude herein that the first prong of the "public generally',
exception has been met.
"Substantiallv the Same Manner"
Nevertheless, if the commissioner determines that a "significant segment" will be
affected by the decision, he must then determine whether the decision will affect his
property in substantially the same manner as it will affect the significant segment.
Pursuant to section 87703, the official must consider the "financial effect" that the
decision will have.
In assessing how a decision can financially affect real property, the Commission
decided during its Phase 2 Conflict of Interest Regulation Improvement Project that
distance.alone is not determinative of whether property is affected.in substantially the
same manner. Therefore, it is possible that houses located a distance beyond 500 feet
from the railroad block could be affected by the decision in substantially the same
manner as the commissioner's property. Commissioner Ferreira-Pro must make this
determination based on information available to him since, as previously noted, the
Commission is not a finder of fact. (See Oglesby, supra.)
In addition to the general rule of regulation 18707.1noted above, there is a special
rule provided by regulation 18707.4 for appointed members of board.s and commissions:
"(a) For the purposes of Government Code section 87103,
the 'public generally' exception applies to appointed
members of boards and commissions who are appointed to
represent a specific economic interest, as specified in
section 87103(a) through (d), if all of the following apply:
3 Please note that while regulation 18707.1(bX1XB) provides that a "significant segment,, can be
10%o or more of all properfy owners or all homeowners in the official's district,this language was adopted
to address a city council member's representation of his or her constituents and specifrcally applies to
election districts. (Memorandum to Commissioners regarding '?roposed Adoption of Amendment to
Regulation 18703, August 23,1993; see also Commission Minutes ofMeeting, September 2, lgg3.)
Consequently, this district language does not apply to Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's situation.


FileNo. A-03-ll0
Page No. 9
(1) The statute, ordinance, or other provision of law
which creates or avthoizes the creation of the board or
commission contains a finding and declaration that the
persons appointed to the board or commission are
appointed to represent and further the interests of the
specifi c economic interest.
(2) The member is required to have the economic interest
the member represents.
(3) The board's or commission,s decision does not have a
reasonably foreseeable_mateial financial effect on any
other economic interest held by the mernber, other than the
economic interest the member was appointed to represent.
(a) The decision of the board or commission will
financially affect the mernber,s economic interest in a
manner that is substantially the same or proportionately the
same as the decision will financially affect a significant
segment of the persons the member was appointed to
represent. For purposes of this regulation, a significant
segment constitutes fifty percent of the persons the mernber
was appointed to rqrresent.
(b) In the absence of an express finding and declaration
or requirernent of the types described in2 Cal. Code Regs.
section I 87 07 .a@)( I ) and Q), the .public generally,
exception only applies if such a finding and declaration or
requirement is implicit, taking into account the language of
the stafute, ordinance, or other provision of law creating or
' :' '.' authorizingthe.creationoftheboardorcomrnission,the .) ., 
.. i:i i.-r!.,
nature and purposes of the program, any applicable
legislative history, and any other relevant circumstance.,,
(Regulation 18707.4.)
Taking into account various sections of Ordinance No. 890, it is implicit that
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro was appointed to represent residents of the historic district.
Therefore, amending the ordinance to include an express finding and declaration
regarding the persons that the commissioner is appointed to represent does not appear to
be necessary for application of regulation 18707.4, so that subdivision (aXl) is met.
Since the commissioner has an interest in real property as previously addressed,
subdivision (a)(2) is also met. Additionally, based on the information you have provided,
the commissioner's real property interest will be affected by the decision. Therefore,
subdivision (aX3) is also met.
FinallS under subdivision (aX4), a significant segment constitutes 5Oo/o of the
persons that Commissioner Ferreira-Pro was appointed to represent. Assuming, again,
that the 1,568 lots in the district that you have identified are residential p.operty owned
by separate owners, the decision must affect 784 of those lots in substantiaily tle same


FileNo. A-03-110
Page No. l0
manner in order to determine whether the 'public generallt''exception applies. The
commissioner must make this factual determination.
If the 1,568 lots are not all residential lots, amending the ordinance to change the
representative from a "resident" to a 'holder of a property interest, including ownership
or leasehold interest" will change the applicable "significant segment" under regulation
18707.4. We cannot assess the consequences of such a change.
Step Eight: The "Legally Required Participation, Exception
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "regally required
participation" rule, an exception to the conflict-of-interest rules, is applicable to either of
the commissioner's situations.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (e16)
322-5660.
Enclosures
NB je
I:\Adviceltrs\03-110
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
May Commissioner Ferreira-Pro participate in a decision regarding an advisory
recommendation on how the railroad block in the historic district should be developed if
his residence is located within 500 feet of the railroad block?
sections.conclusion:
It is presumed that Commissioner Ferreira-Pro has a conflict of interest in this
decision.

sections.analysis:
The primary purpose for the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act is to ensure
that "[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perforrn their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests ofpersons who have supported them." (Section 81001O).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under section 87100 requires
analysis of the following questions as outlined below.2

making, partieipating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the historic district in the City of Folsom, Jeff Ferreira-Pro is a
"member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local govemment agenct'' and,
therefore, is a public official subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.
(Section 82048; regulation 1 870 1 (a).)
' These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at regulation 18700(b).
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (See regulation 18702.1.)
commissioner Ferreira-Pro will "make a goveflrmental decision" if he
participates in a decision regarding an advisory recoilrmendation on how the railroad
block in the historic district of Folsom should be developed. Additionally, ifhe engages
in any of the actions described in enclosed regulations 18702.2 and 18702.3 with regard
to this decision, he will "participate in making" or'tnfluence', that decision.
Step Three: What is Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest - 
the possible
source ofa conflict ofinterest?
Section 87103 provides that apublic official has a "financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation
18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,
or holds any position of management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));
A public,official has an economic interest in real property in which he or.she.has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation
18703.2);
a
o
A public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision (section 87 I 03 (c); regulation 1 3703.3);
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $340 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section
87 1 03(e); regulation 187 03.4);
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances,
including those of his or her immediate family -- this is the "personal financial
effects" rule (section 87I03; regulation 13703.5).
An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the
spouse of an official orby a mernber of the official's immediate family, by an agent on
behalf of a public official, or by a business entity or trust in which the official, the
official's immediate family, or their agents own directly, indirectly, or beneficially a
1O-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) "Immediate family''is defined at section
82029 as an official's spouse and dependent children.
Provided he has an interest of $2,000 or more in his residence, the commissioner
has an economic interest in this property.
You have not provided information regarding any other economic interests of
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's. For purposes of this letter, we assume that he has no other
economic interests relevant to the decisions you have identified.

involved in the goyernmental decision?
Real property is directly involved in a govemmental decision if that real property
is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject ofthe governmental decision. (Regulationlg704.2talti).)
You have stated that the decision is regarding the future development of the
railroad block. As such, the railroad block area is the subject of the decision, and the
commissioner's property will be directly involved in the decision since it is located
within 500 feet of this area.
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
If the real property in which an official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision, the materiality standards of regulation rclfiS.Z(a)
apply. (Regulation 187 04.2(cX 1 ).)
Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property which is directly involved in the govemmental decision is
presumed to be material. "This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not
reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on
the real property." (Ibid.) Please note that "any financial effect" includes as little as a
penny's worth.
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of the governmental
decision upon Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest will meet the
applicable materiality standard?
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibllity. Qn re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
It is presumed that the financial effect of this decision on the property owned by
the commissioner is material and that he has a conflict of interest in this decision. As a
result, he is prohibited from participating in this decision unless he can rebut this
presumption or if an exception applies.
In order to rebut the presumption of regulation 18705.2(a)(1), Commissioner
Ferreira-Pro must show thatitis not reasonably foreseeable that the decision (i.e., the
decision to develop the railroad block) will have any financial effect on his property.
However, glven the purposes specified in Ordinance No. 890 pertainjng to the historic
district, it appears that it is unlikely that this presumption will be rebutted.
However, the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providin g advice. (In
re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) Therefore, the determination of whether or not it is
reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standard will be met for
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's economic interest is necessarily afact:ual question that is
ultimately for the commissioner to decide.
Step Seven: The "Public Generally" Exception
Even if a public official determines that his or her economic interest will
experience amateial financial effect as a result of the decision before the official, he or
she may still participate if the "public generally'' exception applies to each person or real
property triggering a conflict of interest. (Regulation 18707@)(4).) Regulation 18707.1
provides the requirements for the general exception:
"(a) Except as provided in Government Code sections
87102.6 and 87103.5, the material financial effect of a
governmental decision on apublic official's economic
interests is indistinguis,lpple from its effect on the public
generally if both subdivisions (b)(1) and (b)(2) of this
regulation apply.
(b) Significant Segments and Indistinguishable Effects.
(1) Significant Segment. The governmental decision will
afFect a 'significant segment' of the public generally if any
of the following are affected as set forth below:
(A) Individuals. For decisions that affect the personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of a public official or
a member of his or her immediate family, or that affect an
individual who is a source of income or a source of gifts to
a public official, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of the population in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the
official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction.
(B) Real Property. For decisions that affect a public
official's real property interest, the decision also affects:
(i) Ten percent or more of all property owners or all
homeowners in the jurisdiction of the official's agency or
the district the official represents; or
(ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency.
(C) Business Entities. For decisions that affect a business
entity in which a public official has an economic interest
the decision also affects 2,000 or twenty-five percent of all
business entities in the jurisdiction or the district the
official represents, so long as the effect is on persons
composed of more than a single industry, trade, or
profession. For pu{poses of this subdivision, a not for
profit entity other than a govemmental entity is treated as a
business entity.
fl...11
(2) Substantially the Same Manner: The govemmental
decision will affect a public official's economic interest in
substantially the same manner as it will affect the
significant segment identified in subdivision (b)(l) of this
regulation."
Sienificant Seqrnent
Regulation 18707.1(b)(1XB) is the "significant segment" standard used when the
decision affects an official's real property. As stated above, to qualiff as a significant segment
under regulation 18707.1O)(1XB), the decision has to also affect "[t]en percent or more of all
property owners or all homeowners in thejurisdiction of the official's agency or the diskict the
official represents; or (ii) 5,000 property owners or homeowners in the jurisdiction of the
official's agency."
We have previously advised that aplanning commissioner's jurisdiction is the
city. (In re owen (1976) 2 FPPC aps.77; Furth Advice Letter, No. A-99-035; Mertng
Advice Letter, No. I-91-091.) In the Owen opinion, supra,the Commission assessed
whether the "public generally''exception applied to several city council members and
planning commissioners, statingthat the phrase "public generally'' was comprised of the
residents and persons doing business in the City of Davis.
Because all residents of the jurisdiction are constituents of the official, these
residents in the agency's jurisdiction comprise the "public ." (In re Legan (1985) 9 FppC
Ops. 1.) In addition, the Commission concluded that, with regard to county supervisors,
the "public" would consist of the entire county, even though its primary land use
jurisdiction is confined to the unincorporated areas of the county. (In re Legan (1985) 9
FPPC Ops. l.)
You have provided information that the City of Folsom created the HDC and that
the general rule for membership eligibility on the commission includes heing a resident


FileNo. 4-03-110
and registered voter in the City of Folsom. In addition, if a member of the commission
moves his or her residence outside of the city, the member will be removed from office.
Furthermore, members of the historic district are required to file a statement of economic
interests under the city's conflict of interest code. Based on these facts, we conclude that
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's jurisdiction is the City of Folsom.
As a result, the commissioner must analyze whether the "prrblic generally''
exception applies based on the jurisdiction ofthe City of Folsom.3 You have stated that
the historic district encompasses approximately 1,568 lots. Assuming that each of these
lots is a residential lot owned by a different property owner and that each lot will be
affected by the decision reTatingto the historic district, the first prong (i.e., the
"significant segment" prong) will be met if this number amounts to 10Yo of all property
owners or homeowners in Folsom. You have not provided facts showing that this is the
case. Therefore, we cannot conclude herein that the first prong of the "public generally',
exception has been met.
"Substantiallv the Same Manner"
Nevertheless, if the commissioner determines that a "significant segment" will be
affected by the decision, he must then determine whether the decision will affect his
property in substantially the same manner as it will affect the significant segment.
Pursuant to section 87703, the official must consider the "financial effect" that the
decision will have.
In assessing how a decision can financially affect real property, the Commission
decided during its Phase 2 Conflict of Interest Regulation Improvement Project that
distance.alone is not determinative of whether property is affected.in substantially the
same manner. Therefore, it is possible that houses located a distance beyond 500 feet
from the railroad block could be affected by the decision in substantially the same
manner as the commissioner's property. Commissioner Ferreira-Pro must make this
determination based on information available to him since, as previously noted, the
Commission is not a finder of fact. (See Oglesby, supra.)
In addition to the general rule of regulation 18707.1noted above, there is a special
rule provided by regulation 18707.4 for appointed members of board.s and commissions:
"(a) For the purposes of Government Code section 87103,
the 'public generally' exception applies to appointed
members of boards and commissions who are appointed to
represent a specific economic interest, as specified in
section 87103(a) through (d), if all of the following apply:
3 Please note that while regulation 18707.1(bX1XB) provides that a "significant segment,, can be
10%o or more of all properfy owners or all homeowners in the official's district,this language was adopted
to address a city council member's representation of his or her constituents and specifrcally applies to
election districts. (Memorandum to Commissioners regarding '?roposed Adoption of Amendment to
Regulation 18703, August 23,1993; see also Commission Minutes ofMeeting, September 2, lgg3.)
Consequently, this district language does not apply to Commissioner Ferreira-Pro's situation.


FileNo. A-03-ll0
(1) The statute, ordinance, or other provision of law
which creates or avthoizes the creation of the board or
commission contains a finding and declaration that the
persons appointed to the board or commission are
appointed to represent and further the interests of the
specifi c economic interest.
(2) The member is required to have the economic interest
the member represents.
(3) The board's or commission,s decision does not have a
reasonably foreseeable_mateial financial effect on any
other economic interest held by the mernber, other than the
economic interest the member was appointed to represent.
(a) The decision of the board or commission will
financially affect the mernber,s economic interest in a
manner that is substantially the same or proportionately the
same as the decision will financially affect a significant
segment of the persons the member was appointed to
represent. For purposes of this regulation, a significant
segment constitutes fifty percent of the persons the mernber
was appointed to rqrresent.
(b) In the absence of an express finding and declaration
or requirernent of the types described in2 Cal. Code Regs.
section I 87 07 .a@)( I ) and Q), the .public generally,
exception only applies if such a finding and declaration or
requirement is implicit, taking into account the language of
the stafute, ordinance, or other provision of law creating or
' :' '.' authorizingthe.creationoftheboardorcomrnission,the .) ., 
.. i:i i.-r!.,
nature and purposes of the program, any applicable
legislative history, and any other relevant circumstance.,,
(Regulation 18707.4.)
Taking into account various sections of Ordinance No. 890, it is implicit that
Commissioner Ferreira-Pro was appointed to represent residents of the historic district.
Therefore, amending the ordinance to include an express finding and declaration
regarding the persons that the commissioner is appointed to represent does not appear to
be necessary for application of regulation 18707.4, so that subdivision (aXl) is met.
Since the commissioner has an interest in real property as previously addressed,
subdivision (a)(2) is also met. Additionally, based on the information you have provided,
the commissioner's real property interest will be affected by the decision. Therefore,
subdivision (aX3) is also met.
FinallS under subdivision (aX4), a significant segment constitutes 5Oo/o of the
persons that Commissioner Ferreira-Pro was appointed to represent. Assuming, again,
that the 1,568 lots in the district that you have identified are residential p.operty owned
by separate owners, the decision must affect 784 of those lots in substantiaily tle same


FileNo. A-03-110
Page No. l0
manner in order to determine whether the 'public generallt''exception applies. The
commissioner must make this factual determination.
If the 1,568 lots are not all residential lots, amending the ordinance to change the
representative from a "resident" to a 'holder of a property interest, including ownership
or leasehold interest" will change the applicable "significant segment" under regulation
18707.4. We cannot assess the consequences of such a change.
Step Eight: The "Legally Required Participation, Exception
The facts you have presented do not suggest that the "regally required
participation" rule, an exception to the conflict-of-interest rules, is applicable to either of
the commissioner's situations.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Commissioner Ferreira-Pro participate in a decision regarding an advisory
recommendation on how the railroad block in the historic district should be developed if
his residence is located within 500 feet of the railroad block?

CONCLUSION: It is presumed that Commissioner Ferreira-Pro has a conflict of interest in this
decision.
embedding.first_500_words: Farn PoLrrrcAL PnecrrcEs CorraMrssroN 428 J Street. Suite 620. Sacramenro, CA 95914-2329 (916) 322-5660. Fax (916) 322-0886 August 22,2003 Bruce C. Cline Office of the CityAttomey City of Folsom 50 Natoma Street Folsom, CA 95630 Re: Your Request for Advice Our FiIe No. A-03-110 Dear Mr. Cline: This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Historic District Commissioner Jeff Ferreira-Pro regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 QUESTTON May Commissioner Ferreira-Pro participate in a decision regarding an advisory recommendation on how the railroad block in the historic district should be developed if his residence is located within 500 feet of the railroad block? CONCLUSION It is presumed that Commissioner Ferreira-Pro has a conflict of interest in this decision. FACTS The Historic District Commission ("HDC") is an appointed commission that serves as the planning commission for the historic district in Folsom. It is governed by Folsorn Municipal Code Chapter 11.52. The HDC is responsible for land use decisions in a specifically defined areathat encompasses approximately 1,568 lots covering I Government Code sections 81000 - gl0l4. Commission regulations appeff at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code ofRegulations. if4rg9 File No. 4-03-110 Page No. 2 approximately 98 blocks. The membership of the HDC is defined by ordinance. The ordinance requires the commission to be comprised of the following: "A. One representative actively involved with historic preservation; B. One representative who is a resident of the Historic District; C. One representative who owns a business within the Sutter Street Subarea; D. Two Planning Commissioners; E. One representative from a Historic District business outside the Sutter Street Subarea; and F. One architect, landscape architect, or design professional with expertise in historic preservation. " Commissioner Ferreira-Pro is presently the chair of the HDC, nominated as the chair by his fellow commissioners. He sits on the HDC by virtue of his residence, in which he owns a single family home in the historic district; as such, he is the appointed "representative who is a resident of the historic district." The commissioner's residence is within 500 feet of a portion of alarge parcel of city property known as the railroad block. The railroad block is largely undeveloped and presently used for parking. There are a number of proposals regarding how the railroad block will be developed and these proposals will be presented to the HDC for advisory recofilmendations before a final decision by the city council. In some cases,land use actions necessary for development of the railroad block may be presented to the HDC for final action. The railroad block comprises an area of approximately 6.7 acres. The cost of development in this area will be millions of dollars. The city, the HDC and the people who live and work in the historic diskict place significant importance on this project. It is a very important economic development project for the city and important.to the historic character of the city. The railroad block project and proposed uses are
